abstract:
Large "instruction-tuned" language models—those fine-tuned to respond to natural language commands—have shown a remarkable ability to generalize to new tasks in a zero-shot setting. However, the performance and breadth of these models are heavily constrained by the quality and quantity of human-written instruction data, which is often limited in scale, diversity, and creativity. To address this bottleneck, we introduce SELF-INSTRUCT, a framework that aligns pretrained language models with natural language instructions by bootstrapping from their own generations. 

Our pipeline works by generating new instructions, alongside corresponding input and output samples, directly from a language model. It then filters out low-quality or redundant entries before using the refined synthetic dataset to fine-tune the model itself. When applied to a vanilla GPT-3 model, SELF-INSTRUCT yields a 33% absolute improvement on the SUPER-NATURALINSTRUCTIONS benchmark, achieving performance nearly equal to InstructGPT-001—a model trained on proprietary user data and extensive human annotations. 

To further validate our approach, we curated a set of expert-written instructions for novel, user-oriented tasks. Human evaluation demonstrates that tuning GPT-3 with SELF-INSTRUCT significantly outperforms models trained on existing public instruction datasets and closes the performance gap with InstructGPT-001 to within 5%. SELF-INSTRUCT provides a virtually annotation-free method for aligning language models with human intent. We are releasing our dataset of 52K synthetic instructions and our evaluation suite to facilitate future research in instruction tuning.
